response.description_long:
Engineering teams that merge dozens of times a day pay a tax on every merge: a CI pipeline that is slow, occasionally flaky, and debuggable only by scrolling raw logs. Pipewright's answer is to treat the build as production infrastructure — every pipeline run on GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, or Jenkins becomes a searchable trace showing which jobs ran, what they cost, which tests flaked, and where the queue backed up.

The mechanics are deliberately light. A collector — a single static binary — runs as a step in the existing pipeline configuration, with no agent daemons and no proxy, streaming timing, cache hits, artifact sizes, and per-test results parsed from JUnit, Jest, pytest, and Go test output. A typical build absorbs 2 to 4 seconds of overhead, and the collector fails open if the backend is unreachable. On the other end, a columnar store keeps 13 months of history, and jobs become spans in a waterfall view borrowed from application tracing.

Two features carry the platform-team pitch. Flake detection replays failing tests in isolation on ephemeral runners, up to three times, marking the nondeterministic ones and feeding a leaderboard of the tests that waste the most engineer-hours. Cost attribution meters every job minute against its runner class and rolls spend up by repository, team, and pipeline — the site's claim is that a handful of pipelines usually account for the majority of spend, half of it cache misses.

The customers named are platform teams at Brightloop, Kestrelworks, and Fernrock Labs. Brightloop cut median merge-to-green time from 41 minutes to 14 by resharding its monorepo build on critical-path data; Kestrelworks bills CI back to game teams from 400-job nightly builds; Fernrock Labs quarantined 61 flaky tests in a month. The target buyer is a company between 20 and 2,000 engineers.

Pricing is a conventional ladder: free for open-source projects and up to 3 developers, a Team plan at $30 per developer per month with the full 13 months of history, and an Enterprise tier adding SSO, audit logs, self-hosted runner support, and a dedicated Slack channel.

Pipewright was founded in 2022 in Seattle, WA by Dana Whitfield and Arjun Mehta, two ex-infrastructure engineers who ran build systems at a large e-commerce company; the team is 14 people, remote-first. The competitive framing is do-it-yourself: against Grafana dashboards and homegrown scripts, Pipewright argues build telemetry is a product, not a side project — the schema, flake detector, and cost attribution come ready, and the collector installs in under ten minutes.
